title: The nonsense of Xueqin Jiang's "predictive history"
full_text: The attempt to link Trump's war with Iran to the Sicilian expedition is clever sounding, but in historical terms, it is thin, forced, and mostly wrong. The comparison flatters the pundit far more than it explains the war. It takes one famous ancient disaster, strips away its structure, ignores its personalities, forgets its chronology, and then pastes the label onto a modern crisis because the phrase America's Sicilian expedition sounds rather grand. It is not serious history, nor is it reasonable prediction. If you want to gaze in a teacup and look at tea leaves, you should probably go and consult a a Turkish astronomer in some way uh area of back some backwater of Istanbul. Um pay her um a few um a few Turkish lura and sit back and enjoy the theater because that is all it is. The first problem is simple. The Sicilian expedition was not merely a story about a greater power attacking a distant enemy and getting into trouble. That is far too vague to mean much. It is I if that is the standard then half of military history becomes another Sicily. The real story of 415 to 413 B.CE is more specific. Athens launched a huge expedition to Sicily in order to subdue Syracuse and expand imperial power westward. And the enterprise was bound up with divided leadership, strategic overreach, delay, and above all the disastrous removal of alabiades, who then defected and advised Sparta, the Athenian enemy, to aid Syracuse and fortified Declare. Those elements are central, not decorative. Lose them and one loses the analogy completely. There is no defecting enemy in the story of Trump's um Iranian expedition. And that is why the line um that that that that is why this prediction uh from a an obscure Chinese astrologer who's presented as the new Nostradamus is Cosswallop. There is no Alabiades here and that matters so much. Alabades is not an optional flourish. He is at the heart of the drama. He is the dazzling unstable brilliant individual at the at the center of the story. Um he he is the one whose ambition helps launch the expedition, whose recall tears the command structure apart, whose defection sharpens the Spartan response. Uh, if you remember Mary Renault's book, The Last of the Wine, which I remember reading years ago, the late Athenian world is made vivid partly because figures such as Alibabades embody the moral and political instability of the age. Renault's novel is set in the last phase of the pelpeneisian war and treats that world as one shaped by charisma, volatility, os, betrayal, and the collapse of civic confidence. To mention Sicily without grasping alabi is to miss the human engine of the story. And Trump's Iran war has no such figure. There is no recalled architect of the campaign. Um there is no single magnetic aristocrat whose removal shatters command coherence in the theater of war. There is no equivalent to Athens sending out a divided triumvirate then crippling itself by political self- sabotage at the opening of the campaign. One may dislike Trump. One may distrust his judgment or criticize the operation and there's ample ground for all three. Yet none of that produces an alkaviades. And without that figure, the analogy loses its main support. The second problem is the strategic form. And it's worth looking uh for for a little bit at the story itself. Uh I' I've I've got I've got I've got some little pictures here, some watercolors which I think might entertain. Um and Athens in Sicily was conducting a massive expeditionary war aimed at conquest, occupation, siege, and political domination far from home. The present Iran war is, at least so far, centered on air strikes, maritime coercion, and strategic um missile attacks, uh mining, shipping disruption pressure around the straight of Hormuz. Since February the 28th, the conflict has been defined by bombardment, Iranian retaliation, the effective closure of Homo's allied reluctance to join the effort, and a struggle at sea lanes and energy flows. This is not Syracuse. This is not a siege city campaign with circumventation walls, cavalry imbalance, and an invading force trapped in a harbor after failed ground operations. Uh it it it it may be a situation where a larger enemy attacks and is then foiled uh by by its own grandeur, its own um it it's it its own uh it its own pomposity and that may be the resemblance that that is the narrow point and only one narrow point. Both cases raised the danger of narrow of of naval or maritime defeat in an constrained environment. Athens was eventually trapped and broken in the great harbor of Syracuse and uh the United States now faces a maritime problem in and around Hormuz where Iran has reportedly laid mines, struck shipping and imposed major costs on movement through one of the world's key energy choke points. If a if a commentator wishes to say this recalls the danger of a superior power finding its sea power blunted in restricted waters, then that is fair enough. Yet that is slender resemblance and the uh and and and and it and it's not it's not not enough. So even even on its own terms, Sicily is not a tidy parable of failed expedition equals immediate imperial death. The war's later phases still mattered. Athens suffered, adapted, and continued. And that point alone should make anyone cautious about using Sicily as a quick prophecy machine. Uh the ancient record points instead to a long aftermath, deeper instability, harder fighting, slower reckoning, and that is a far far messier thing than uh some uh nondescript um individual from China making some sort of pseudo prophecy. The current war also has um many modern features that uh that that that are that do not fit the Sicilian mold at all. NATO states and other allies. Um they uh the the uh NATO states and other allies have joined have declined to join in the mission in Hormuz. Poland has said it will not send forces. Trump has publicly lashed out against allies while also claiming the United States doesn't need these forces. Um well, you know, the the the United States, I think, probably does need uh support and and uh clearly isn't getting it. Um so people shouldn't point to Syracuse, Nikkias or Alavades. They should point to Washington in 2026. And the judgment I think is plain. The Sicilian expedition is the wrong master analogy for this war. It is too dependent on a vanishing or vanished political setting uh too bound to the singular role of alqai too rooted in a campaign of conquest and siege rather than one of strikes and sealane disruption. The only modest point of contact lies in the danger of maritime humiliation in restricted waters and beyond that the comparison collapses. If one wants Mary Rena in the background then use her properly. The last of the wine helps one feel the late Athenian mood. Um the ambition, the glamour, the faction, the betrayal and the loss. It reminds us why Alabiades was not a footnote but a force of personality who made and unmade strategy through charm and treachery. I had a rabbit called Alabiades. By the way, remove alabi and one is no longer reading the same story. That is why the analogy fails. No alabi, no true Sicily. What sits before us is not an American replay of 415 BCE. It is a modern maritime and aerial crisis danger enough dangerous enough on its own terms without blad uh classical analogies to make it sound um vaguely uh v vague vaguely impressive. And it doesn't really sound very impressive at all. the Chinese professoring Jang uh who's got this popular YouTube channel which I think is called predictive history uh is is is is basing his predictions on Hokum and he may he may indeed accurately have predicted the return of Donald Trump to the US presidency that doesn't require a lot of tea staring Um that simply requires a a roll of the dice frankly. Um and it was predictable in many ways. Um and uh the the the um the the the idea that the Iranian adventure will be a disaster again doesn't require that much um predictive ability. But it but but it but it is an interesting analogy. I think it's a mistaken analogy and uh I I hope I've managed to demonstrate some of that um and to lay waste the to to to lay to lay to um to to bed the nonsense put forward by this um predictive history individual. We can learn from history but not in this way. Not not not not in not in that way. We can learn from history by looking at history and looking at the dynamic of the of the interaction of people and activities. But what happened in the past is not repeated in the present. Um what happened in the past may be informed may inform the present and may help us to judge better how to respond to events. But um I I I I have as much time for for tea leaf um starers as I have for people who think that the book of revelation is some sort of coded um uh co coded prediction of what is going to happen in the next few years. that this millinarian bug is a sickness.
